topic: Someone finds out their best friend is moving across the country
raw_output:
[story 1]

The glossy flyer lay crumpled on the coffee table, the city names blurring under my gaze. Liam’s name, printed in bold, seemed impossibly far away. I traced the outline of the map with a fingertip, the paper crinkling beneath my touch. A hollow ache settled in my chest, a heavy, dull pressure that made it hard to draw a full breath. I picked up my phone, intending to call, but the words caught in my throat, turning to dust. I just stared at the screen, watching the little notification icons flicker, unable to summon the energy to type a proper message. The silence in the apartment suddenly felt vast, amplifying the quiet emptiness where his presence usually resided.

[story 2]

I watched the moving truck pull away, its massive shadow shrinking against the horizon. My shoulders slumped, a slow, heavy surrender to the reality sinking in. Every muscle in my body felt weighted down, like I’d swallowed a handful of gravel. I walked to the window, pressing my forehead against the cool glass, trying to absorb the view, but the scenery just swam into an indistinct smear of color. It wasn't just a change of address; it was the severing of a daily fixture, a sudden, jarring shift in the landscape of my life. A single tear escaped, tracing a hot, slow path down my cheek before I quickly wiped it away with the back of my hand, embarrassed by the sheer vulnerability of the reaction.

[story 3]

When he finally admitted it, his voice was light, almost breezy, which only made the news land harder. I felt a strange numbness spread through my limbs, as if the connection to him had been abruptly severed by an invisible wire. I stood by the door, my hands gripping the frame so tightly my knuckles turned white. The air in the room seemed thin, insufficient for the weight of what I was processing. I looked around my familiar space—the worn armchair, the stack of shared books—and everything seemed overly bright, painfully vibrant against the gray feeling pooling in my core. I just nodded, a small, jerky movement, and felt a profound inability to articulate anything beyond a weak, breathy, "Okay."

I traced the outline of the map with a fingertip, the paper crinkling beneath my touch. A hollow ache settled in my chest, a heavy, dull pressure that made it hard to draw a full breath. I picked up my phone, intending to call, but the words caught in my throat, turning to dust. I just stared at the screen, watching the little notification icons flicker, unable to summon the energy to type a proper message. The silence in the apartment suddenly felt vast, amplifying the quiet emptiness where his presence usually resided.

I watched the moving truck pull away, its massive shadow shrinking against the horizon. My shoulders slumped, a slow, heavy surrender to the reality sinking in. Every muscle in my body felt weighted down, like I’d swallowed a handful of gravel. I walked to the window, pressing my forehead against the cool glass, trying to absorb the view, but the scenery just swam into an indistinct smear of color. It wasn't just a change of address; it was the severing of a daily fixture, a sudden, jarring shift in the landscape of my life. A single tear escaped, tracing a hot, slow path down my cheek before I quickly wiped it away with the back of my hand, embarrassed by the sheer vulnerability of the reaction.

When he finally admitted it, his voice was light, almost breezy, which only made the news land harder. I felt a strange numbness spread through my limbs, as if the connection to him had been abruptly severed by an invisible wire. I stood by the door, my hands gripping the frame so tightly my knuckles turned white. The air in the room seemed thin, insufficient for the weight of what I was processing. I looked around my familiar space—the worn armchair, the stack of shared books—and everything seemed overly bright, painfully vibrant against the gray feeling pooling in my core. I just nodded, a small, jerky movement, and felt a profound inability to articulate anything beyond a weak, breathy, "Okay."
